summary: SLOs serve as an abstraction layer that protects engineering teams from micromanagement, priority hijacking, and feature pressure by establishing clear boundaries and shared ownership of service quality. When stakeholders sign off on SLOs, they agree that reliability is the top priority, giving teams leverage to push back on disruptions and justify technical debt work. The framework works bidirectionally—teams that exceed their SLOs should stop polishing and ship features.
key_points: SLOs establish a perimeter around engineering teams that prevents unauthorized interference from management and stakeholders
Getting stakeholders to sign off on SLOs makes them co-owners of production excellence, not just feature demanders
Error budgets provide a common language for evaluating risk without deeply understanding every individual situation
SLOs work bidirectionally: they justify reliability work when budgets are tight and push teams to ship when budgets are healthy
The real value of SLOs is organizational, not just technical—they formalize trust between teams
money_quotes: SLOs are a powerful weapon to wield against micromanagers, meddlers, and feature-hungry PMs.
Senior management might not understand that fifty thousand things a day are broken, and the team cannot stop and fix or care about every single one of them.
When you are exceeding your SLO you can stand to add more chaos to the system, so turn the knob back up.
SLOs are the right level of abstraction for agreements between teams within an org for the same reasons they are useful between companies.
Instead of having to deeply understand and evaluate the risk of every single situation in its own unique glory, we have a simple common language for evaluating risk in terms of error budgets.
key_insight: SLOs are not primarily a technical tool for measuring uptime—they are an organizational contract that defines how other teams and managers are allowed to interact with your engineering team.
